name: Contact Deduplication System
scenario: You're building a contact-management tool for a CRM system (think Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho CRM). Sales reps add contacts constantly, and duplicates creep in: same person added with slightly different details, same phone number with different email addresses, etc. Your job: build the deduplication engine that identifies and merges duplicates. This requires PROPERLY implementing equals/hashCode — which is exactly the topic you just learned. CRM dedup logic is real production code worth millions to companies (data quality is a key business asset).
description: Build a `Contact` class with full Object method overrides, then a `ContactBook` that detects duplicates. Demonstrates real-world use of equals/hashCode for finding duplicates and managing collections of objects.

core.description: `Contact` class with private fields: name (String), email (String), phone (String)
Constructor takes all three; validates none are null/empty
Override equals: two contacts are equal if EMAIL matches (case-insensitive) — emails are the unique identifier
Override hashCode: based on email (lowercase to match equals)
`ContactBook` class with private Contact[] contacts field and an int count of how many are actually filled
Constructor takes initial capacity (Contact[] of that size, count = 0)
`addContact(Contact c)`: if a duplicate (by email) already exists, print 'Duplicate ignored' and don't add. Otherwise add.
main() demonstrates: adding 5 contacts (some duplicates), final count, printing all contacts

bonus.description: Add `findByEmail(String email)`: returns Contact or null. Uses equals for matching.
Add `removeByEmail(String email)`: removes contact if found. Returns boolean for success.
Add a `mergeContact(Contact incoming)` method: if email matches existing contact, UPDATE that contact's name and phone with new values (the latest data wins). Otherwise add as new.
Add `countByDomain(String domain)`: count contacts whose email ends with @domain

data_to_use:
Test scenario in main():

1. Create ContactBook with capacity 10

2. Try to add 5 contacts:
   - Aisha Khan, aisha@gmail.com, 9876543210
   - Raj Patel, raj@company.com, 8765432109
   - Aisha K., AISHA@GMAIL.COM, 9876543210  ← duplicate (case-insensitive email match)
   - Priya Sharma, priya@yahoo.com, 7654321098
   - Raj Patel Sr., raj@company.com, 8765432109  ← duplicate

3. Print: count of unique contacts (should be 3), all contacts via toString

4. Bonus operations if implemented:
   - findByEmail('priya@yahoo.com') → returns Priya's contact
   - countByDomain('gmail.com') → 1
   - removeByEmail('raj@company.com') → true, count = 2
   - Try removeByEmail('notfound@x.com') → false
example_output:
===================================
      CONTACT DEDUPLICATION
===================================
Added: Contact[name=Aisha Khan, email=aisha@gmail.com, phone=9876543210]
Added: Contact[name=Raj Patel, email=raj@company.com, phone=8765432109]
Duplicate ignored: aisha@gmail.com
Added: Contact[name=Priya Sharma, email=priya@yahoo.com, phone=7654321098]
Duplicate ignored: raj@company.com

Final count: 3

All contacts:
Contact[name=Aisha Khan, email=aisha@gmail.com, phone=9876543210]
Contact[name=Raj Patel, email=raj@company.com, phone=8765432109]
Contact[name=Priya Sharma, email=priya@yahoo.com, phone=7654321098]

(Bonus) Find priya@yahoo.com:
  Contact[name=Priya Sharma, email=priya@yahoo.com, phone=7654321098]
(Bonus) Gmail.com count: 1
(Bonus) Removed raj@company.com: true
(Bonus) Final count: 2
===================================

criteria: hashCode uses same field (email lowercase) (5 pts)
addContact uses equals to detect duplicates (15 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. The key design choice: equals compares emails CASE-INSENSITIVELY. This means: (1) equals lowercases both, OR (2) the field is stored lowercased on creation. Either approach works. hashCode must use the SAME comparison logic.
submission_instructions: Save as ContactDedup.java. Run it. Push to GitHub portfolio. This is REAL CRM deduplication logic — Salesforce/HubSpot/Zoho all have similar engines (with more sophisticated fuzzy matching).
tips_for_success: For case-insensitive email equality: use `this.email.equalsIgnoreCase(other.email)` in equals
Or simpler: in constructor, store email lowercased. Then equals can use normal Objects.equals.
Whatever equals does, hashCode must match. If equals lowercases for comparison, hashCode must use the lowercase value.
addContact: loop through current contacts (0 to count-1), check if any .equals() the new one
Don't iterate to contacts.length — many slots are null. Iterate to count.
For removeByEmail: find index, shift remaining contacts down, decrement count, set last slot to null.
mergeContact: find existing by email, if found update name/phone (need setters or new constructor), if not add new
concepts_reinforced: Array management with count tracking (pre-ArrayList pattern)
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this:
Beyond practicing Object method overrides, you build REAL CRM functionality. Deduplication is a HUGE pain point in data systems — bad CRM data costs companies millions. Your engine uses email as the unique key (case-insensitive — because users type 'aisha@gmail.com' and 'Aisha@Gmail.COM' interchangeably).

The equals/hashCode contract becomes practical here: if you put these Contacts in a HashSet (next module), your dedup logic happens automatically. The custom array-based dedup you're writing now will be one-line `Set<Contact> uniqueContacts = new HashSet<>(allContacts)` later. But understanding the underlying mechanism is essential.

Real CRMs go further: 'fuzzy matching' (Levenshtein distance for typos), phone number normalization (strip non-digits), name variants ('Robert' = 'Bob'). But the FOUNDATION is exactly what you're building.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add fuzzy email matching (ignore dots in local part: john.smith@gmail.com = johnsmith@gmail.com)
Normalize phone numbers (strip spaces, dashes, +91 prefix)
Implement contact merging that COMBINES info (longer name wins, both phones kept)
Add a 'last updated' timestamp to track when contacts were modified
Convert internal storage from Contact[] to HashSet<Contact> when Collections module hits — dedup becomes automatic
Add Levenshtein distance check for name typos
